claims.claim_text: The opening airline scaling incident gives a dated, named-industry story with precise traffic, startup-time, and revenue-loss figures.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In December 2020, a major US airline was running its booking system on a fleet of EC2 instances managed by a custom deployment pipeline. During a holiday travel surge, traffic spiked 3x beyond projections. The operations team scrambled to launch new instances, but the bootstrapping process -- installing dependencies, pulling container images, registering with the load balancer -- took 8 minutes per instance. For 8 minutes, customers saw timeout errors. Bookings were lost. The post-incident review estimated $2.3 million in missed revenue during that single scaling event.
claims.suggested_rewrite: During a major traffic surge, teams that scale by bootstrapping new EC2 instances can still spend several minutes bringing fresh capacity online. That lag can translate into timeouts and lost bookings before new capacity is ready.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with fake-precise timing and revenue numbers.

claims.claim_text: The follow-up success story gives a precise migration timeline and scale-out timing for the same unnamed airline anecdote.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: They migrated to ECS on Fargate six weeks later. The next holiday surge came, and Fargate launched new containers in under 15 seconds. No EC2 instances to bootstrap. No AMIs to maintain. No capacity planning for unpredictable peaks. The traffic spike was 4x that year. Nobody noticed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Moving a containerized service to ECS on Fargate removes the need to bootstrap EC2 instances for each scale-out event, reduces AMI maintenance, and can make sudden traffic surges easier to absorb.
claims.rationale: This keeps the same unsourced anecdote and precise timeline/performance claims.

claims.claim_text: Amazon ECS is AWS's managed container orchestration service, and AWS Fargate is its serverless compute option for containers.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 3
claims.rationale: AWS's ECS FAQ directly describes ECS as the managed orchestration service and Fargate as the serverless compute option.

claims.claim_text: Task definitions are versioned, and each new revision increments the revision number within a task family.
claims.span_hint: section: ECS Architecture Overview -> Core Concepts
claims.rationale: The ECS TaskDefinition API reference explicitly documents task definition revisions.

claims.claim_text: A task can contain multiple containers that communicate over localhost because containers in the task share task networking.
claims.span_hint: section: ECS Architecture Overview -> Core Concepts
claims.rationale: AWS documents that containers in the same Fargate task can communicate over the localhost interface.

claims.claim_text: Fargate pricing is billed per second with a one-minute minimum and is based on requested vCPU, memory, storage, OS, and architecture.
claims.span_hint: section: EC2 Launch Type vs Fargate table, Pricing row
claims.rationale: AWS's pricing page directly states the billing dimensions and per-second billing model.

claims.claim_text: The launch-type comparison table presents Fargate cold start as a fixed 45-90 second number.
claims.span_hint: section: EC2 Launch Type vs Fargate table, Startup time row
claims.anchor_text: | Startup time | Seconds (on warm instances) | 45-90 seconds (cold start: microVM + ENI + image pull) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Startup time | Seconds (on warm instances) | Often longer than EC2 warm starts because Fargate must provision isolated compute and pull the container image |
claims.rationale: AWS documents the startup components, but not a universal fixed 45-90 second range that will hold across images and environments.

claims.claim_text: Fargate tasks do not support the `gpu` task definition parameter.
claims.span_hint: section: EC2 Launch Type vs Fargate table, GPU support row
claims.rationale: AWS explicitly lists `gpu` among the task definition parameters that are not valid for Fargate tasks.

claims.claim_text: Fargate supports task sizes up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory.
claims.span_hint: section: EC2 Launch Type vs Fargate table, Max task size row
claims.rationale: AWS's task CPU/memory documentation includes the 16 vCPU and 120 GB upper bound.

claims.claim_text: Fargate Spot pricing can be up to 70% lower than regular Fargate pricing.
claims.span_hint: section: EC2 Launch Type vs Fargate table, Spot support row
claims.rationale: AWS's pricing page directly states the 'up to 70%' discount claim.

claims.claim_text: Fargate allows only specific task-level CPU and memory combinations rather than arbitrary pairings.
claims.span_hint: section: CPU and Memory Combinations for Fargate
claims.rationale: AWS publishes the allowed CPU and memory combinations for Fargate tasks.

claims.claim_text: The task execution role is for the ECS/Fargate agent, while the task role is for application code inside the container.
claims.span_hint: section: IAM Roles: Execution Role vs Task Role
claims.rationale: AWS's IAM role overview distinguishes the execution role from the application task role.

claims.claim_text: Services that use Fargate with `awsvpc` networking must use load balancer target groups of type `ip`.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating a Service with ALB Integration, target group note
claims.rationale: AWS's Fargate guide explicitly says Fargate services with `awsvpc` mode must use `ip` target type.

claims.claim_text: The module states as a universal rule that production ECS tasks should never have direct internet exposure.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating a Service with ALB Integration, assignPublicIp note
claims.anchor_text: **`assignPublicIp: DISABLED`**: Tasks in private subnets. They reach ECR through a VPC endpoint or NAT Gateway. This is the production pattern -- never expose tasks directly to the internet.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **`assignPublicIp: DISABLED`**: A common production pattern is to place tasks in private subnets, let them reach ECR through VPC endpoints or a NAT gateway, and expose traffic through a load balancer instead of giving each task a public IP.
claims.rationale: AWS documents the private-subnet pattern, but the absolute 'never' security rule is too strong as written.

claims.claim_text: With `maximumPercent: 200` and `minimumHealthyPercent: 100`, ECS can launch up to twice the desired count while preserving the full healthy count; calling that automatically 'zero-downtime' is too absolute.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating a Service with ALB Integration, deployment percentages note
claims.anchor_text: **`maximumPercent: 200, minimumHealthyPercent: 100`**: During deployments, ECS can launch up to 200% of desired count (6 tasks if desired is 3) while keeping 100% healthy. This means zero-downtime rolling deployments.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **`maximumPercent: 200, minimumHealthyPercent: 100`**: During deployments, ECS can launch up to 200% of desired count (6 tasks if desired is 3) while keeping 100% healthy, which is a common configuration for minimizing or avoiding user-visible interruption during rolling deployments.
claims.rationale: AWS documents the scheduler behavior, but 'zero-downtime' is an operational outcome, not a guaranteed property of those settings alone.

claims.claim_text: The circuit-breaker note mixes a real rollback feature with an incorrect release year and a blanket 'enable on every service' recommendation.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating a Service with ALB Integration, deploymentCircuitBreaker note
claims.anchor_text: **`deploymentCircuitBreaker`**: If new tasks keep failing, ECS automatically rolls back to the last working version instead of endlessly retrying. This was added in 2021 and should be enabled on every service.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **`deploymentCircuitBreaker`**: If new tasks keep failing, ECS can automatically mark the deployment as failed and roll back to the last completed deployment. The feature became generally available in late 2020.
claims.rationale: AWS documents the rollback behavior and the late-2020 GA date, but not the universal recommendation.

claims.claim_text: Amazon ECS service discovery integrates with AWS Cloud Map for DNS-based service discovery.
claims.span_hint: section: Service Discovery, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: AWS's service discovery documentation directly describes ECS service discovery as using AWS Cloud Map and DNS namespaces.

claims.claim_text: With ECS service discovery, DNS records resolve to task private IP addresses rather than the load balancer.
claims.span_hint: section: Service Discovery, after create-service example
claims.rationale: AWS documents that service discovery DNS records register the task private IP address and route traffic to the task, not the load balancer.

claims.claim_text: ECS Exec uses AWS Systems Manager Session Manager and lets you access containers without opening inbound ports or managing SSH keys.
claims.span_hint: section: ECS Exec: Debugging Running Containers, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: AWS's ECS Exec guide directly describes the Session Manager architecture and the no-SSH/no-inbound-ports behavior.

claims.claim_text: The task role needs `ssmmessages` permissions for ECS Exec.
claims.span_hint: section: Prerequisites for ECS Exec
claims.rationale: AWS's task IAM role guide lists the required `ssmmessages` actions for ECS Exec.

claims.claim_text: CodeDeploy blue/green deployments for ECS require two target groups and a production listener, with an optional test listener.
claims.span_hint: section: Blue/Green with CodeDeploy
claims.rationale: The CodeDeploy ECS deployment workflow explicitly lists the required listeners and target groups.

claims.claim_text: Fargate Spot tasks run on spare capacity and can be interrupted with a two-minute warning.
claims.span_hint: section: Fargate Spot: Saving Up to 70%, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: AWS's Fargate guide directly documents spare-capacity usage and the two-minute interruption warning.

claims.claim_text: The ECS/Kubernetes chronology sentence is internally inconsistent and should be corrected rather than cited as written.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1 opening sentence
claims.anchor_text: **ECS predates Kubernetes' public release.** Amazon launched ECS in April 2015, just months after Kubernetes 1.0 was released in July 2015.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **ECS predates Kubernetes' public release.** Amazon ECS became generally available in April 2015, and Kubernetes 1.0 was released in July 2015.
claims.rationale: The original sentence says ECS predates Kubernetes 1.0 while also saying ECS launched months after it, so it needs correction.

claims.claim_text: The module makes unsupported market-share and internal-usage assertions about ECS versus EKS and Amazon.com's retail platform.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 1 remaining sentences
claims.anchor_text: While Kubernetes won the industry mindshare war, ECS runs more containers on AWS than EKS does. Many of the largest AWS customers -- including Amazon.com itself -- use ECS internally rather than Kubernetes. AWS's own retail platform processes millions of transactions using ECS.
claims.suggested_rewrite: ECS remains a major AWS container platform alongside EKS, and AWS highlights it as a service used for large-scale internal and customer workloads.
claims.rationale: These are specific comparative and internal-usage claims without an allowlisted primary source that directly supports them as written.

claims.claim_text: AWS says Firecracker powers both AWS Lambda and AWS Fargate, and that a microVM can launch in as little as 125 ms.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 2
claims.rationale: The AWS Firecracker announcement directly states both service relationships and the 125 ms figure.

claims.claim_text: `awsvpc` gives each task its own ENI and private IP, so security groups apply at the task level rather than the host level.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 3
claims.rationale: AWS's Fargate networking guide directly documents per-task ENIs, private IPs, and task-level networking behavior.

claims.claim_text: The ECS Exec history anecdote combines a real release date with unsupported popularity and GitHub-reaction claims.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, item 4
claims.anchor_text: **ECS Exec was one of the most requested features in ECS history**, with the GitHub issue gathering hundreds of thumbs-up reactions over three years before it was finally released in March 2021. Before ECS Exec, debugging a Fargate container required adding SSH servers to your container images (a terrible security practice) or relying entirely on logs. The feature uses SSM Agent embedded in the Fargate platform, so you do not need to add anything to your container image.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **ECS Exec was released in March 2021** and lets you run commands in running containers without opening inbound ports or managing SSH keys. On Fargate, the feature uses AWS-managed SSM components, so you do not need to add your own SSH server to the image.
claims.rationale: The March 2021 launch is citable, but the popularity/history claims are not supported here by an allowlisted primary source.

further_reading.title: AWS Fargate for Amazon ECS
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative overview of Fargate capabilities, Spot behavior, platform versions, and load balancing requirements.

further_reading.title: Amazon ECS Task Networking Options for Fargate
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers ENIs, private IPs, public IP assignment, NAT gateways, and VPC endpoint patterns.

further_reading.title: Monitor Amazon ECS Containers with ECS Exec
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains ECS Exec architecture, prerequisites, IAM implications, logging, and operational limits.

further_reading.title: CodeDeploy Blue/Green Deployments for Amazon ECS
further_reading.why_relevant: Details the ECS blue/green model, target-group requirements, listener setup, and deployment behavior.

notes: Quiz items, hands-on exercise steps, code blocks, and purely instructional prose were excluded per task rules. Table claims were included only where they carried specific pricing, benchmark, size, or vendor-behavior assertions.
